video_title: Why AI Might Actually Create More Work for Lawyers | Odd Lots
gist: Gary Wingens, chair of law firm Lowenstein Sandler, argues AI is cutting the cost of individual units of legal work (e.g., a due-diligence project fell 70% in cost) while a Jevons-paradox dynamic expands total legal work volume, keeps hourly rates rising, and leaves firms still deciding on tech stack, training, and pricing as AI vendors shift from flat to token-based pricing.

insights.claim: Billable-hour pricing in law has persisted for decades despite universal complaints because clients don't fully trust project/value-based quotes and prefer hourly billing so they can see exactly what work was done, according to Lowenstein Sandler chair Gary Wingens.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Explains why alternative fee arrangements have stalled industry-wide for 15+ years despite constant client and lawyer complaints — a structural friction investors in legal-tech should account for.
insights.quote: I have never met a client who has come to me asking to buy billable hours.

insights.claim: Average hourly rates at the largest U.S. law firms rose 10.1% in 2025 per data published by The American Lawyer, with the top 20 firms by profitability rising even faster, far outpacing roughly 3% CPI inflation that year.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A concrete, sourced data point showing elite law firm pricing power is accelerating even as AI compresses hours worked — a signal that top-tier legal services are not being commoditized by AI, unlike lower-value legal work.
insights.quote: average hourly rates in 2025 went up by 10.1% at the largest firms in United States, and at the top, the top 20 firms by profitability, they went up even more

insights.claim: Gary Wingens expects hourly billing rates at top-tier law firms to keep rising faster than in the past because AI tools make each billable hour more productive and valuable, even as the total price clients pay for a given deal or matter falls due to fewer hours being needed.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Implies a bifurcation investors should track: per-hour law firm pricing power rising even as total client legal spend per matter falls — margin dynamics differ from a simple 'AI destroys legal fees' narrative.

insights.claim: Wingens believes AI-driven cost transparency and reduced outcome uncertainty could finally push the legal industry toward project- or outcome-based pricing, a shift that has failed to take hold for the past 15 years of 'alternative fee arrangement' pushes.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A potential structural shift in how legal services are priced and sold, relevant to anyone evaluating legal-tech vendors selling outcome- or workflow-based pricing models.

themes.theme: AI as efficiency tool vs. thought partner

insights.claim: AI is changing corporate legal work in two distinct ways: as an efficiency tool (comparable to black-lining software or Microsoft Word) that lowers the cost of producing a given work product, and, more novel, as a 'thought partner' or copilot that improves the substantive quality of the legal work itself from the outset — a function prior technology waves never provided.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A reusable framework for evaluating any legal-AI product: does it just cut costs (a commodity play) or does it upgrade output quality (a defensible, harder-to-replicate value proposition)?

insights.claim: A due-diligence project reviewing thousands of trust agreements had its cost quoted down by roughly 70% using AI for the first review pass (with lawyers doing QC), turning a project the client had rejected as too expensive (roughly $10 million in the firm's illustrative figures) into one worth doing (roughly $3 million), generating revenue that otherwise would have been zero.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A concrete, quantified case of AI unlocking previously uneconomical legal work rather than just shrinking existing revenue — direct evidence for the Jevons-paradox thesis in legal services.
insights.quote: when the price came down by 70%, they said, oh yeah, at that price, we'll do it

insights.claim: One Lowenstein Sandler tax partner uses Claude to stress-test and generate new international tax-structuring ideas for family-office clients, in an iterative loop where the AI's output is then handed to an associate to research and validate — producing structures Wingens says neither the AI nor the human would have arrived at alone.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Illustrates a human-in-the-loop workflow pattern (AI ideation -> human validation) that specialized professional-services firms are using in practice, relevant to how AI augments (not replaces) senior expert judgment.

insights.claim: Lowenstein Sandler patent lawyers using an AI tool for over six months report the primary benefit is a better patent application, because the AI brings cross-disciplinary scientific and engineering knowledge beyond an individual patent attorney's own specialty (e.g., a biologist's perspective added to a software patent).
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Suggests AI's edge in specialized professional work may come less from speed and more from synthesizing cross-domain knowledge no single human expert holds — a differentiator that's harder to commoditize.

insights.claim: Some of Lowenstein Sandler's most sophisticated clients now send the firm AI-generated first drafts of contracts, complaints, or answers for the firm to refine, while certain patent clients use their own AI tools to review the firm's AI-assisted patent work — meaning client-side and firm-side AI agents are effectively interacting with each other.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Points to an emerging workflow where legal work is increasingly AI-to-AI mediated before human lawyers touch it, with implications for how legal-tech vendors should design interoperability and review layers.

insights.claim: Wingens does not believe current AI models can invent an entirely new structured-finance product (e.g., a novel ABS or CMBS structure) from scratch, though they can meaningfully assist expert lawyers in testing and refining structuring theories they already have in mind.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A useful boundary condition for AI hype in structured finance: treat current models as validators/co-pilots for expert-generated ideas, not as autonomous financial-engineering inventors.
insights.quote: I don't, I don't I don't believe that you can come up with a new structure.

insights.claim: Gary Wingens confirms a Jevons-paradox dynamic in legal services: cutting the cost of a specific legal task (such as reviewing trust agreements) can turn previously uneconomical work economical, expanding the total volume of legal work performed rather than simply shrinking law-firm revenue per matter.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Core thesis for evaluating legal-tech and law-firm economics: falling per-unit AI-driven costs may grow the addressable market for legal services rather than compress law firm revenue.

insights.claim: If AI-driven efficiency brings electronic-discovery costs down from roughly $2 million to $200,000 per matter, it would make sophisticated litigation dramatically cheaper to file — a change Wingens frames as good for access to justice (e.g., civil-rights plaintiffs) but also likely to increase overall litigation volume, including from large corporates that previously abandoned claims as uneconomical.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A specific, tradeable-adjacent forecast: falling e-discovery costs could expand litigation volume industry-wide, a tailwind for litigation-focused law firms and legal-tech e-discovery vendors alike.
insights.quote: If we can bring, say, 2 million of discovery costs down to 200,000, it does change the calculus for, both. The good news lawsuits are a lot more economical to file.

insights.claim: A Lowenstein Sandler patent client whose engineers now use AI internally has seen the number of inventions and internal patent requests quadruple, which Wingens expects to offset the falling per-application cost of patent prosecution with much higher total application volume.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that AI-driven R&D productivity gains at clients can flow through as higher demand for downstream legal/IP services, a second-order beneficiary effect of enterprise AI adoption.
insights.quote: they're seeing a quadrupling in the number of inventions that they're that their engineers and scientists are coming up with, and the number of patent requests that they're getting internally from their engineering team has quadrupled

insights.claim: Host Joe Weisenthal argues the Jevons paradox could apply broadly to bureaucracy: AI-armed insurers already push back against claims while AI-armed claimants push back against insurers, risking an escalating volume of automated back-and-forth that consumes rather than saves aggregate effort.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal, host
insights.so_what: Warns that AI-driven productivity gains in adversarial, high-friction domains (insurance, litigation, HR) may not translate into net productivity at the economy level, a caution against assuming AI = pure efficiency gain.
insights.quote: the future could just be a bunch of bots, like talking to each other and filing different claims

themes.theme: Legal AI tech stack: Harvey, security, and build-vs-buy

insights.claim: Lowenstein Sandler is primarily a 'Harvey shop,' with almost all of its lawyers using the Harvey legal-AI platform regularly, alongside Microsoft Copilot and the Office/Outlook suite, rather than lawyers interacting directly with frontier models like Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Direct evidence of enterprise legal-AI vendor adoption patterns at a mid-size big-law firm, useful for tracking Harvey's competitive position against direct frontier-model access and rivals like CoCounsel/Westlaw.

insights.claim: The core value application layers like Harvey add over calling a frontier model directly is a more robust security and ethics layer — controlling where client data goes (including offline sessions), preventing loss of attorney-client privilege that can occur when lawyers use consumer-grade Claude or ChatGPT, plus shareable internal 'playbooks' and a legal-tuned retrieval-augmented-generation layer for tasks like finding supporting quotes across deposition transcripts.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Clarifies the actual moat for vertical legal-AI application layers versus raw frontier-model access: security/privilege protection and shared institutional knowledge, not just model quality.

insights.claim: Wingens does not believe Lowenstein Sandler, or even a much larger firm like Kirkland & Ellis, will train its own foundation model from scratch, citing Kirkland's reported plan to spend roughly $500 million over five years on AI infrastructure and estimating training a frontier model costs on the order of $1.5 billion — instead the firm plans to build customized playbooks and fine-tuned layers on top of existing models.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Sets a cost benchmark showing why even large law firms will remain customers of frontier-model providers and application layers rather than competitors, supporting continued demand for platforms like Harvey.
insights.quote: Does a Kirkland announce they're spending $500 million over five years, right. It costs something like a billion and a half dollars to train a model.

insights.claim: Enterprise legal-AI pricing has so far been offered as unlimited 'all you can eat' plans, but vendors are shifting to token-based pricing — Wingens notes Claude is doing this first — and law firms don't yet know the true, unsubsidized cost of their AI usage, creating risk of a 'sticker shock' repricing once outside-investor-subsidized pricing normalizes.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A direct warning for anyone modeling legal-tech or AI-vendor margins: current enterprise AI pricing may be subsidized and due for a repricing event that could reverse some of the cost savings firms are currently booking.
insights.quote: we don't know the true cost, especially if we, you know, kind of hit this, energy grid wall

insights.claim: Wingens' own Claude usage cost $36 across the first three business days of July, which he characterizes as trivial relative to the value of billable time, illustrating how small current marginal AI usage costs look under today's (possibly subsidized) pricing.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A real, if small, individual data point on current AI usage costs at a law firm, useful as an anchor before comparing against future token-based pricing once subsidies fade.
insights.quote: so far this month I've my use of Claude has used up $36

insights.claim: Lowenstein Sandler has not reduced hiring of summer associates or first-year lawyers and has already filled its summer-associate class for 2027, though the firm says it is shifting the skill set it screens candidates for.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Direct counter-evidence, from inside a big-law firm, against the narrative that AI is already shrinking junior legal hiring — relevant to assessing law-school employment and junior-associate labor market fears.

insights.claim: Junior-lawyer training at Lowenstein Sandler is shifting away from tedious hands-on tasks like manual black-lining toward AI-assisted, simulator-style training where trainees use AI tools live during sessions and gain access to the firm's full historical database of negotiated deal terms instead of relying on informal hallway knowledge-sharing.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A concrete model of how AI is restructuring professional apprenticeship in law, relevant to any professional-services firm rethinking how it trains junior staff once routine work is automated.

insights.claim: Wingens is genuinely unsure whether removing repetitive 'grunt work' (the legal equivalent of a musician practicing scales) will impair young lawyers' development of the attention to detail and judgment that the old apprenticeship model built, calling it an unresolved behavioral-psychology problem for the profession.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Flags a genuine, unresolved talent-development risk in AI-augmented professional services that could affect the long-term quality pipeline of expert judgment across knowledge industries, not just law.

insights.claim: Wingens suggests that rising 'superstar' lawyer compensation (comparable to sports-style signing bonuses) may partly reflect firms paying to capture a top lawyer's proprietary deal knowledge into firm-wide AI systems and playbooks, since AI tools only add value if partners are willing to contribute their expertise — creating a cultural tension at highly autonomous law firms between individual partner independence and firm-wide AI value creation.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Identifies a non-obvious driver of rising elite professional-services compensation and a real alignment risk for any firm trying to build AI knowledge systems on top of autonomous, highly compensated experts.

insights.claim: Wingens expects large clients with dedicated in-house legal teams to keep more routine, high-volume legal work in-house as AI automates it — citing how most banks already brought ISDA and derivatives documentation in-house — while outside firms retain value where broad, cross-client market knowledge and independent perspective matter most.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A structural forecast for how AI could reshape the outside-counsel business model, shifting commoditized routine work toward in-house teams while premium, market-breadth advisory work stays with law firms.

themes.theme: Malpractice risk and regulatory posture reversal

insights.claim: Malpractice insurers (Lowenstein Sandler works with underwriters out of Lloyd's of London) have flipped their posture on AI in roughly two years — from asking whether lawyers were being allowed to use AI at all (a risk concern) to now asking whether lawyers are being required to use AI properly, mirroring how corporate clients went from banning AI use 18 months ago to now demanding it to cut costs.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: Signals that AI use in legal work has crossed from optional/risky to an expected baseline in under two years, which should accelerate legal-AI vendor adoption curves and shift malpractice liability norms toward non-use of AI being the riskier choice.

insights.claim: Despite widespread AI adoption, fabricated ('hallucinated') case citations slipping into court filings remain a real and embarrassing risk that Wingens says has no excuse given the verification tools now available, underscoring that AI legal output still requires mandatory human double-checking.
insights.speaker: Gary Wingens, chair of Lowenstein Sandler
insights.so_what: A persistent operational risk for any firm or legal-tech vendor deploying generative AI in filings — verification/QC layers remain a mandatory cost center, limiting how far headcount reduction can go.
insights.quote: there was no excuse for filing a brief or pleading in a court that cites, you know, made up hallucinated cases. They're just embarrassing.
